title: The Vespers Journal
author: L. K. Voss
description: Dr. Elara Voss's private journals, recovered after her mysterious disappearance, reveal the truth behind the Cordova Plague and the famous Voss Serum. But the truth is not a simple tale of heroic science — it is a confession of mass murder, a fabricated crisis, and a love affair with a man who never existed.

content.text: The Cordova Plague of 2087 changed the world. It emerged without warning in the low-rise hab-blocks of Cordova, a fungal pathogen that liquefied the lungs and turned the skin a bruised, necrotic purple. Within weeks, the quarantine zone was a charnel house. The World Health Authority, mired in bureaucracy, could offer nothing but palliative drones and prayer. And then, from the chaos, a single name rose: Dr. Elara Voss, an independent biochemist whose experimental serum — derived from a rare deep-sea lichen — cured every patient it touched. She was hailed as a saviour. Statues were built. The Voss Serum became mandatory planetary prophylaxis. Voss herself vanished three months after the cure was distributed, leaving behind a single data cache that has remained sealed until today. The cache contains her private journal. The truth it reveals is not a story of heroism. It is a confession.

The following pages are the complete transcription of the journal, with contextual commentary by the WHA Historical Division. We present them in their original, unflinching chronology. The woman in these pages is not the saint of the public memorials. She is the architect of the horror she ended, and the lover of a phantom she created to justify it all.

January 12, 2087. I have made a decision. The WHA will never approve the lichen trials in time. Too expensive, they say. Too risky. The fungal blooms have been growing in the underlevels for years, and they know it. I have given them every projection, every simulation. Without intervention, a pathogenic strain will jump to humans within five years. Five years is an eternity to them. To me, it is a countdown. I will not let my life's work rot in a data silo while millions die.

The plan is audacious, and monstrous. I will not lie to myself. I will engineer a strain of the fungus that is transmissible and deadly but susceptible to the serum I have already developed. I will release it in a controlled, isolated population. The outbreak will force the WHA's hand. They will beg for a cure. I will give it to them. I will save thousands by sacrificing a few dozen. The math is cold, but clear. I have chosen Cordova. It is poor, overcrowded, and already has a high incidence of unexplained respiratory illness. No one will look too closely.

February 3, 2087. The pathogen is ready. But a cure cannot simply appear. It needs a narrative, a drama, a hero and a villain. I am a researcher, not a performer. So I have created a fictitious colleague: Dr. Kael Thorne. He is brilliant, charismatic, and my opposite in every way. I have crafted a digital trail of his existence — forged academic credentials, fabricated research logs, even a handful of low-resolution photos generated by the AI. He is, in the world's eyes, the true discoverer of the serum, but also a dangerous radical who locked his work away out of paranoia. I will play the role of the outsider who must convince him to release it.

I feel a strange affection for Thorne. He is the person I might have been if I had not become this cold, calculating creature. In my journal, I will refer to him as if he were real. I must inhabit the fiction completely. When the outbreak begins, I will release fabricated communications showing Thorne's resistance. I will leak hints of a secret lab. The world will see a conflict between two scientists — one selfish, one selfless. And when I finally 'persuade' Thorne and the serum is deployed, the narrative will be irresistible. The villain will disappear, a convenient mystery. The hero will emerge. And the cure will be trusted because it was fought for, not handed down.

March 22, 2087. It is done. I released the aerosol this morning in the ventilation shafts of Hab-Block 17. The first cases will present within seventy-two hours. I watched them from a rooftop across the sector, the people going about their morning, children heading to a dilapidated school, vendors firing up their griddles. I felt nothing. No — I felt a terrible, quiet awe at my own capacity for detachment. This is the price. I will bear it.

The symptoms are exactly as I designed: a deep, bloody cough, purpura, rapid lung consolidation. By the time the WHA emergency response kicks in, I will already have the serum prepared. But I will not release it yet. I need the death toll to be high enough to create a global panic, but not so high that the quarantine is breached. I am walking a razor's edge. I sleep in two-hour increments, and in my dreams, Thorne speaks to me. He is disappointed. He tells me I could have found another way. I scream at him that he is not real. He simply smiles, and I wake with wet cheeks.

chapters.title: Chapter 5 — The Journal: The Hero's Rise

content.text: April 5, 2087. The world is in chaos. Seventeen confirmed dead in Cordova, and the quarantine has expanded to the entire sector. I have leaked the first of the Thorne files, showing his early work on a fungal inhibitor. The media is hungry, and the name 'Thorne' is already trending. I step forward as his former colleague, offering to help the WHA locate him. I play the role of the earnest, desperate scientist. It is not difficult. The fear in my voice is real, just not for the reasons they assume.

I have set up the final act. I will lead a WHA team to a fabricated location — an abandoned medical lab on the outskirts of Cordova — where we will 'discover' Thorne's serum and his notes. The notes will depict him as a man tormented by guilt, who hid the cure out of fear of its misuse. I will have to fake his death, or his disappearance, to close the narrative loop. I am constructing a ghost. And the world will love him more than they could ever love a living woman who simply did the math.

April 20, 2087. The serum has been deployed. The recovery rate is unprecedented. I am a hero. The WHA has given me a medal. But I cannot sleep. Thorne is everywhere — in the news, in the grateful whispers of survivors, in the faces of the families who do not know their loved ones were my sacrifices. The lie has grown beyond me. I have created a man who is better than I am, and I have fallen in love with him, and I am the only one who knows he is a void.

I am writing this final entry before I destroy the pathogen stockpiles and delete all digital traces of my crime. But I cannot destroy this journal. It is the only place where the truth lives, and where my guilt has weight. I will seal it and leave it to be discovered after I am gone. I do not ask for forgiveness. I only ask that you remember: the woman they built statues for was the same woman who released the plague. And the man they mourned as a tragic genius never drew a single breath. I am both the hero and the villain. And the only absolution I can imagine is this: the cure is real. The saved are real. The dead are real too. I carry them all. I am the only one left who does.

Elara Voss disappeared on April 22, 2087. Her shuttle was found adrift in the Outer Belt, the airlock open, the cabin empty. The official report cited a probable suicide. The search for Dr. Kael Thorne was quietly abandoned. The Voss Serum continues to be manufactured and administered to this day, credited to the 'Thorne-Voss Collaboration.' The Cordova Plague is studied as a case of heroic epidemiology, and the memorial in the central plaza of Cordova bears the names of the dead and the image of a man who never existed. This journal is the only truth. It is placed now in the public record with a recommendation for restricted access. The legacy of Elara Voss is a paradox that may never be resolved. But the dead are still dead. And the living owe them the honesty of this confession.

content.alt: An empty lab with a single sealed journal on a metal table
content.caption: The data cache that contained the journal, left in a derelict shuttle.
